main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Kashmiri Pulao is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a full plate of fragrant saffron-tinted rice garnished with dry fruits and accompanied by mushrooms or mixed vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Pulao (vegetable pulao / pilaf), cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports values for vegetable pulao (cooked basmati rice with ghee, spices, and vegetables). Kashmiri Pulao adds dry fruits and saffron, which modestly increase energy and sugar. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN vegetable pulao baseline with adjustment for dry-fruit inclusion (~10% by weight), consistent with standard recipe proportions. Per-100g values reflect the composite cooked dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — basmati rice, cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: Kashmiri Pulao is based on cooked basmati rice. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of approximately 50–58 for cooked basmati rice (mean ~54). Addition of ghee, dry fruits, and spices slightly lowers the effective GI due to fat and fibre content. GI of 54 is used as a conservative analogous estimate anchored to basmati rice GI data. GL per serving = (54 × 26.8 × 300/100) / 100 = 43.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg/100 g) and use of ghee
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving contributes approximately 630 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced salt preparation, as cumulative sodium from rice dishes can approach 25–30% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (54) and high glycemic load per serving (~43.5)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although basmati rice has a lower GI than many other rice varieties, the large serving size (300 g) produces a high glycemic load of approximately 43.5; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g and pair with a protein or high-fibre side dish to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The ghee used in Kashmiri Pulao contributes saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and consider requesting preparation with a minimal amount of oil rather than ghee to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kashmiri Pulao is a fragrant, mildly spiced rice dish originating from the Kashmir Valley in northern India. It is prepared by cooking long-grain basmati rice in ghee with whole aromatic spices such as cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaves, then finishing with saffron-infused water to impart a golden hue and floral aroma. The dish is characteristically garnished with fried dry fruits — raisins, cashews, and almonds — which provide sweetness and textural contrast. Variants include wild mushroom pulao, where earthy mushrooms are folded into the rice, and mixed vegetable pulao, where seasonal vegetables such as peas, carrots, and beans are incorporated. Unlike biryani, Kashmiri Pulao is not layered and is typically milder in spice intensity, making it suitable for a broad palate. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy (ghee) and tree nuts. Nutritionally, a 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 504 kcal, 9.6 g protein, 15.3 g fat, and 80.4 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is approximately 54 (low–moderate), anchored to basmati rice GI data, but the glycemic load per serving is high (~43.5) due to portion size. Clinically, the dish warrants portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease, primarily due to its high carbohydrate load and saturated fat from ghee. Sodium content is moderate and should be monitored by those with hypertension.

confidence_reason: No exact Kashmiri Pulao entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN vegetable pulao with adjustment for dry-fruit inclusion. GI is estimated from basmati rice GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN vegetable pulao baseline; Kashmiri Pulao adds dry fruits (~10% by weight) and saffron, modestly increasing energy and sugar relative to plain vegetable pulao. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients except ghee, which contains negligible B12; confirmed zero for practical purposes. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in vegetable pulao prepared with ghee at standard quantities. 6. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on cooked basmati rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 7. The input food_name 'Kashmiri Pulao / Wild Mushroom / Veg Pulao' reflects menu variants at Zaffron Tandoori, Boat Quay, Singapore; canonical name normalised to 'Kashmiri Pulao' with variants captured in aliases. 8. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per G6 rules for Indian cuisine served in Singapore.
